title: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Epidemiology and Burden of Pancreatic Diseases
items.content: Pancreatic disorders, including acute and chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic cysts, and pancreatic cancer, present significant management challenges and impose a high burden on healthcare resources globally. Acute pancreatitis is a common cause for hospitalization in gastroenterology, while chronic pancreatitis affects quality of life due to pain and exocrine insufficiency. Pancreatic adenocarcinoma has persistently high mortality rates.
keyPoints: Acute pancreatitis: ~33.7 cases per 100,000 person-years globally, with 1.16 deaths per 100,000 person-years.
Chronic pancreatitis: ~9.6 cases per 100,000 person-years globally, with 0.09 attributable deaths per 100,000 person-years.
Annual prevalence of chronic pancreatitis in the U.S.: 42–73 cases per 100,000 adults.
Healthcare costs for acute and chronic pancreatic diseases: ~$3 billion annually.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Diagnosis of Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis is diagnosed using a combination of laboratory, imaging, and clinical symptoms. Chronic pancreatitis diagnosis is challenging due to nonspecific abdominal pain and the inaccessibility of direct examination. Patients may not have elevated blood amylase or lipase levels, but signs of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI) can indicate disease presence.
keyPoints: Acute pancreatitis: Diagnosed with laboratory, imaging, and clinical symptoms.
Chronic pancreatitis: Diagnosis is difficult due to nonspecific pain; many patients do not have elevated amylase or lipase levels.
EPI in chronic pancreatitis becomes evident when >90% of the pancreas is damaged.
Noninvasive tests (e.g., fecal elastase) are more likely abnormal in advanced disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Incidental Detection and Management of Pancreatic Cysts
items.content: The increased use of cross-sectional imaging has led to a high prevalence of incidentally detected pancreatic cysts. The most common type is intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN), which requires radiographic surveillance in the absence of high-risk features. Mucinous cystic neoplasms occur almost exclusively in women, while serous cystadenomas have a negligible risk of malignancy.
keyPoints: Incidental detection prevalence: 2–5% with CT scans, 20–30% with MRI.
Most common pancreatic cyst type: Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN).
Mucinous cystic neoplasms (MCNs): Occur almost exclusively in women.
Serous cystadenomas: Negligible risk of progression to malignancy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.title: Serum Lipase as the Preferred Test for Acute Pancreatitis Diagnosis
items.content: In diagnosing acute pancreatitis, serum lipase is more specific to the pancreas compared to amylase. A value greater than three times the upper limit of normal (3× ULN) in combination with epigastric pain strongly suggests acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Serum lipase is more specific for the pancreas than serum amylase.
Values > 3× ULN suggest acute pancreatitis when combined with epigastric pain.
Approximately 85% of patients with acute pancreatitis have elevated serum lipase and amylase levels by threefold or greater.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.title: Limitations of Serum Amylase in Pancreatic Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Serum amylase can be elevated due to conditions unrelated to the pancreas, as it is found in various organs and tumors. Isoamylase determinations do not accurately distinguish pancreatic from nonpancreatic sources.
keyPoints: Elevated serum amylase may occur in conditions other than pancreatitis, such as lung, esophageal, breast, and ovarian carcinomas.
Isoamylase tests are not reliable for distinguishing the source of elevated amylase levels.
Macroamylase measurement can help avoid unnecessary testing in patients with unexplained hyperamylasemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Serum Amylase and Lipase Levels
items.content: COVID-19 infection can cause asymptomatic elevations in serum amylase and lipase levels. These elevations are more associated with the severity of COVID-19 rather than acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic elevations of amylase (>3× ULN) and lipase (>3× ULN) have been observed in COVID-19 patients.
Pooled prevalence of hyperlipasemia was 5.6% and hyperamylasemia was 4.0% during the pandemic.
Overall prevalence of acute pancreatitis among these patients was only 1.7%, indicating that elevated levels are more linked to COVID-19 severity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Imaging Modalities in Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Ultrasonography is often the initial imaging modality for suspected pancreatic disease, useful for detecting edema, inflammation, calcification, gallstones, biliary dilation, pseudocysts, and mass lesions. However, its sensitivity can be limited by obesity and bowel gas. CT with intravenous contrast is superior for assessing complications of pancreatitis, such as fluid collections, necrosis, and neoplasms. It should ideally be performed after 72 hours in acute cases to avoid unnecessary risks and costs. MRI and MRCP provide excellent imaging for the bile duct, pancreatic duct, and parenchyma, with specific advantages in evaluating cystic lesions and differentiating necrotic debris.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography is often initial but limited by obesity and bowel gas.
CT with intravenous contrast is best for assessing complications of pancreatitis.
CT should ideally be performed after 72 hours to avoid unnecessary risks in acute cases.
MRI and MRCP provide excellent imaging for bile duct, pancreatic duct, and parenchyma.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Timing of CT Scan in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: CT scans are not recommended within the first three days of confirmed acute pancreatitis to minimize risks such as contrast-induced nephropathy and unnecessary healthcare costs. The major benefit of a CT scan is diagnosing pancreatic necrosis in patients unresponsive to conservative management after 72 hours, as perfusion defects indicative of necrosis may take 48–72 hours to develop.
keyPoints: CT scans are not recommended within the first three days of acute pancreatitis.
Perfusion defects indicative of pancreatic necrosis may take 48–72 hours to develop.
CT is beneficial for diagnosing pancreatic necrosis after 72 hours if unresponsive to conservative management.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Sensitivity of MDCT in Detecting Pancreatic Cancers
items.content: MDCT, utilizing dual-phase scanning with intravenous contrast, has an overall sensitivity for detecting pancreatic cancers ranging from 76–97%. However, its sensitivity is lower for smaller lesions (≤2 cm). Contraindications to using intravenous contrast include renal failure (serum creatinine >2 mg/dL) and severe allergic reactions to iodinated contrast agents.
keyPoints: MDCT has a sensitivity of 76–97% for detecting pancreatic cancers.
Sensitivity is lower for lesions ≤2 cm.
Contraindications include renal failure (serum creatinine >2 mg/dL) and severe allergic reactions to iodinated contrast.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.title: Optimal Timing and Utility of EUS in Pancreatic Disease
items.content: EUS is not beneficial during acute pancreatitis but should be performed after resolution (~4 weeks) to detect predisposing factors such as malignancy or choledocholithiasis. It can be combined with ERCP for diagnosing and managing choledocholithiasis and pancreatic neoplasm with biliary obstruction.
keyPoints: EUS is not beneficial during acute pancreatitis; perform after ~4 weeks.
Detects predisposing factors like malignancy, choledocholithiasis, pancreatic divisum, or ampullary lesions post-acute pancreatitis.
Can be combined with ERCP for diagnosing and managing choledocholithiasis and pancreatic neoplasm with biliary obstruction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.title: EUS Criteria and Sensitivity in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: EUS is a valuable diagnostic tool for chronic pancreatitis, with sensitivity comparable to MRI/MRCP and better than CT. The presence of five or more of nine specific criteria on EUS is highly predictive of chronic pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of EUS: 81% (95% CI, 70–89%)
Comparable to MRI/MRCP sensitivity: 78% (95% CI, 69–85%)
Better than CT sensitivity: 75% (95% CI, 66–83%)
Presence of five or more of nine criteria on EUS is highly predictive of chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with acute pancreatitis leading to hospitalizations?
options: Biliary tract disease or alcohol abuse
items.explanation: Acute pancreatitis is frequently secondary to biliary tract disease or alcohol abuse.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is a common consequence of chronic pancreatitis?
items.explanation: Chronic pancreatitis is associated with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is a significant challenge in diagnosing chronic pancreatitis?
items.explanation: The diagnosis is hampered by the nonspecificity of the associated abdominal pain.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which pancreatic cyst type has a negligible risk of progression to malignancy?
items.explanation: Serous cystadenomas have a negligible risk of progression to malignancy.

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.question: Which enzyme is considered the most specific for diagnosing acute pancreatitis?
items.explanation: Lipase is more specific for the pancreas and strongly suggests acute pancreatitis when elevated.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.question: In which condition might serum amylase levels be spuriously low?
items.explanation: Patients with hypertriglyceridemia and acute pancreatitis may have spuriously low levels of amylase.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.question: What is the recommended screening test for acute pancreatitis in patients with renal disease?
items.explanation: Serum lipase is recommended due to its specificity, despite mild elevations in renal disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.question: What can cause elevated serum amylase levels other than acute pancreatitis?
items.explanation: Elevated serum amylase levels can occur in renal dysfunction, hypertriglyceridemia, and COVID-19 infection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.question: Which imaging modality is considered the best for assessing complications of acute and chronic pancreatitis?
items.explanation: CT with intravenous contrast is the best imaging study for assessing complications of acute and chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.question: What are common sonographic findings in patients with acute pancreatitis?
options: Enlarged pancreas and peripancreatic stranding of fat tissue
items.explanation: Ultrasonography can indicate the presence of gallstones, biliary dilation, pseudocysts, and mass lesions in acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.question: Why is a CT scan not recommended within the first 3 days of confirmed acute pancreatitis?
options: To avoid contrast-induced nephropathy and unnecessary costs
Because it cannot detect pancreatic necrosis early
Due to interference from bowel gas
MRI is preferred during this period
items.explanation: CT scan in the first 3 days of acute pancreatitis is not recommended to minimize risk of contrast-induced nephropathy and unnecessary health care costs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.question: What is the main benefit of using CT scans in acute pancreatitis?
options: Diagnosing pancreatic necrosis in non-responsive patients
items.explanation: The major benefit of CT scan in acute pancreatitis is the diagnosis of pancreatic necrosis in patients not responding to conservative management within 72 h.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.question: When is EUS not beneficial for evaluating the pancreas?
options: After resolution of acute pancreatitis (~4 weeks)
For detecting predisposing factors like malignancy
items.explanation: EUS is not beneficial during acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.question: What is the sensitivity of EUS for diagnosing chronic pancreatitis compared to MRI/MRCP and CT?
options: Lower than both MRI/MRCP and CT
Higher than MRI/MRCP but lower than CT
Comparable to MRI/MRCP and higher than CT
Lower than MRI/MRCP but higher than CT
items.explanation: EUS has a sensitivity of 81%, which is comparable to MRI/MRCP (78%) and better than CT (75%).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.question: Which condition can EUS help manage by delivering nerve-blocking agents?
items.explanation: EUS facilitates the delivery of nerve-blocking agents for pancreatic pain from chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.statement: Acute pancreatitis is one of the least common reasons for hospitalizations in gastroenterology.
items.explanation: Acute pancreatitis is one of the most common reasons for hospitalizations in gastroenterology.

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: The global pooled incidence of chronic pancreatitis is higher than that of acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: The global pooled incidence of chronic pancreatitis is lower than that of acute pancreatitis.

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.statement: Plain films of the abdomen are commonly used to diagnose pancreatic disease.
items.explanation: Plain films rarely provide useful information for pancreatic disease and have been replaced by more detailed imaging studies.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.statement: Elevations in serum lipase >3× ULN can occur due to nonpancreatic etiologies.
items.explanation: Serum lipase elevations can be seen in conditions like hepatobiliary malignancies, septicemia, and liver cirrhosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.statement: MRI is more sensitive than CT scans for detecting choledocholithiasis.
items.explanation: MRI is more sensitive than transabdominal US and CT scans for the detection of choledocholithiasis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.statement: Ultrasonography can always reliably image the pancreas, regardless of patient body habitus.
items.explanation: Obesity and excess intestinal bowel gas can interfere with pancreatic imaging using ultrasonography.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.statement: EUS is preferred over MRI/MRCP and CT for diagnosing chronic pancreatitis due to its higher sensitivity.
items.explanation: EUS has a slightly higher sensitivity (81%) compared to MRI/MRCP (78%) and CT (75%).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)

items.subtopic: Other Abdominal Disorders (part 2)
items.statement: Nonspecific changes seen on EUS can be due to normal aging.
items.explanation: EUS may show nonspecific changes attributable to factors like normal aging.

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: Chronic pancreatitis has a high annual prevalence in the United States.
items.reason: The annual prevalence of chronic pancreatitis is 42–73 cases per 100,000 adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: Noninvasive tests of pancreatic exocrine function are most sensitive in early chronic pancreatic disease.
items.reason: Invasive, direct tests like the secretin stimulation test are more sensitive and specific for detecting early chronic pancreatic disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; invasive tests are more sensitive for early detection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.assertion: Serum amylase levels are always elevated in acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Amylase is found in many organs and can be elevated due to conditions other than pancreatic issues.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because serum amylase may not be elevated if there's a delay in testing or if the underlying disorder is chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE
items.assertion: The measurement of macroamylase can help avoid numerous tests in patients with unexplained hyperamylasemia.
items.reason: Macroamylase determinations accurately distinguish between pancreatic and nonpancreatic sources of elevated amylase.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and macroamylase measurement helps in diagnosing rare disorders causing hyperamylasemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · ■ TESTS USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF PANCREATIC DISEASE

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.assertion: CT scans are the preferred method for evaluating pancreatic mass lesions.
items.reason: MRI with MRCP is more sensitive than CT scans for detecting choledocholithiasis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it pertains to sensitivity for choledocholithiasis, not pancreatic mass lesions.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
items.assertion: EUS-guided fine-needle aspiration is a safer alternative to CT-guided biopsy for pancreatic masses.
items.reason: CT-guided biopsy was commonly used before the advancement of EUS-guided FNA.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why EUS-guided FNA is preferred over CT-guided biopsy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Pancreatic Disease · 1. Pancreatitis | Nonpancreatic Disorders | Other Abdominal Disorders (part 1)
